title: How to apply to integrate new data | Stats NZ
content:
## How to apply to integrate new data
23 August 2022, 12:00pm
If you are interested in applying to provide new data to be added to the IDI/LBD, you will need to follow the following process.

## Step 1 – Expression of interest
When we assess whether datasets should be added to our integrated data products, we consider system-wide need for the data, quality, and logistics of adding data, data ethics and privacy, and resource availability.
While anyone can complete an expression of interest to have data added to the IDI or LBD, the information we require is best provided by the agency that holds the dataset. The expression of interest form asks for information including:
- your organisation’s details
- description of dataset
- linking variable information
- metadata information
- whether your dataset contains ethnicity and/or iwi data.
Once we have received your EOI form, it will be assessed by a member of the Integrated Data Production team and prioritised against a number of criteria. We aim to contact you within two business weeks to discuss next steps for your application.
View the Expression of interest in adding data to the IDI or LBD form .

## Step 2 – Data Ethics and Privacy Assessment
For us to consider adding data to the IDI, it must go through our Data Ethics and Privacy Assessment (DEPA). The DEPA is based on the Ngā Tikanga Paihere framework and Privacy Act 2020 and is important for every data integration project as it helps Stats NZ to:
- assess and mitigate impacts on privacy
- ensure that data is treated in responsible and culturally appropriate ways
- assess if a proposed data integration is consistent with relevant legislation, and consistent with Stats NZ’s data integration principles.
The DEPA form asks for information on:
- the source of the data and collection method(s)
- the original intended use
- wider benefits of the data
- legal and privacy obligations
- cultural responsiveness
- relationship with clients.

## Step 3 – Application reviewed
The Integrated Data production team will assess and discuss with you the practicalities, benefits, and risks of linking the data.
Some of the factors we consider when reviewing applications include:
- How do the potential privacy risks associated with integrating the data compare with the potential value it would create?
- Is there data already in the IDI or LBD that could be used as a proxy for the proposed dataset?

## Step 4 – Prioritising applications
Applications to add data to the IDI or LBD will be prioritised based on system-wide need, quality, and readiness of the data to be linked, and resource availability.
Some of the prioritisation criteria include:
- How will this dataset contribute value to New Zealanders?
- What policy/research issue will this data contribute to?
- How widely used will the data be?
- What is the quality of the variables used for linking?
- Will the data be a one-off supply, or ongoing?
The scale of the linking project will affect the likelihood and/or timing of it being linked. For instance, datasets that need a full integration load take more resources and time than those that require a simple load.
A full integration of data into the IDI or LBD means we link the new population using existing unique identifiers as well as personal-identifying information (for example full name and date of birth) or business-identifying information (for example business name and address).
We perform a simple load (or ‘ad hoc load’) of data when the dataset to be integrated contains a unique identifier that has already been linked in the IDI or LBD. A simple load uses deterministic linking to link a new dataset to an existing population in the IDI or LBD. Projects in this category are typically a one-off supply intended for a specific purpose.
Find the unique identifiers in the IDI that can be used for simple loads in the privacy impact assessment for the dataset: View Privacy impact assessments.

## Step 5 – Endorsement from Data Lab Action Group
The Data Lab Action Group (DLAG) recommends and advises on the priority for adding IDI and LBD datasets when the demand for adding data exceeds our resources.
The DLAG is made up of people from government and non-government sectors, with a mix of knowledge, understanding, and experience of different fields. They provide system-focused advice to Stats NZ on integrated data priorities.

## Step 6 – Data linking preparation and documentation
Once a dataset is scheduled for integration, we prepare for linking and complete important documentation. Requirements and timeframes vary depending on the nature of the project, and the supplier’s availability to assist with these requirements, but can include:
- data sharing agreement – an agreement about what data is supplied and how
- Data Ethics and Privacy Assessment (DEPA) – an assessment of the risks and benefits of adding new datasets
- technical specifications
- metadata (information about the data) that enable users to understand the data. We expect metadata to include a variable-level data dictionary and answers to other specific questions about the dataset.

## Step 7 – Data is transferred to Stats NZ
Once documentation is in place, data is transferred from the supply agency to Stats NZ using secure file transfer systems.

## Step 8 – Data is linked and made available for research
Before we make your dataset available for researchers, we remove personal identifying information such as names, addresses (if these have been supplied), and the day from the date of birth/death. We encrypt (replace with another unrelated number) identifiers such as IRD and NHI numbers.
To access your loaded dataset as part of a Data Lab project, complete the appropriate form, available from either:
How to apply to use microdata for research – new research project How to apply to use microdata for research – change an existing project (variation)
If you have any questions about the data onboarding process or wish to discuss having a dataset added, please contact us at ID-Onboarding@stats.govt.nz
Page updated 23 August 2022
